entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA datacenter revenue (D2) falls sequentially for two consecutive quarters while stated lead times shorten.
entries.mechanism: Memory and packaging suppliers cap how many chips NVIDIA can build, so buyers keep ordering more than it can ship.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether hyperscaler spending holds and to the pace of memory supply.
entries.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
entries.title: NVDA demand durability

entries.falsifiableTrigger: The advanced-packaging supply-demand gap (leadTimes) widens back above 20 percent, or high-bandwidth memory order books (S10) clear before 2028.
entries.mechanism: Memory makers cannot expand high-bandwidth memory fast enough, so that memory sets the ceiling on how many accelerators vendors can ship.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the pace at which memory makers add capacity.
entries.statement: High-bandwidth memory, not advanced packaging, is now the supply that caps how much merchant-GPU revenue vendors can book. Packaging is easing as the TSMC CoWoS gap narrows from about 20% to 10% by end 2026 and ASE and Hanmi add capacity, and NVIDIA's supply commitments run to $119.0B-$145B, while HBM3E/HBM4 stay sold out through 2027 with demand far above supply.
entries.title: Supply constraint binding

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AMD accelerator share of the AI market (market-share-pct) fails to rise above 7 percent through 2026, or its datacenter revenue (D2) growth stalls for two quarters.
entries.mechanism: Large buyers are committing gigawatts of AMD accelerators, which turns AMD's roadmap into real deployed datacenter capacity.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the gigawatt commitments convert to shipments and to AMD's packaging allocation.
entries.statement: AMD converts its Instinct roadmap into material datacenter share.
entries.title: AMD credible second source

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Custom-ASIC share of AI-chip shipments (market-share-pct) stalls below 27.8 percent, or its 44.6 percent growth rate drops under merchant-GPU growth during 2026.
entries.mechanism: Cloud giants build their own accelerators that grow faster than merchant GPUs, so a rising share of new AI compute skips buying NVIDIA and AMD chips.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the growth gap between custom chips and merchant GPUs.
entries.statement: Hyperscaler custom silicon meaningfully displaces merchant-GPU demand growth.
entries.title: Custom ASIC substitution

entries.falsifiableTrigger: H100 hourly rental prices (D6) or secondary resale prices (gpuSpotPrice) fall further quarter over quarter as Blackwell supply ramps.
entries.mechanism: As new-generation supply ramps, buyers move off older chips, so rental prices for the older ones slide even while the newest stay firm.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the pace of the Blackwell supply ramp and older-chip resale.
entries.statement: GPU rental/spot price levels hold up despite added supply.
entries.title: Pricing power persistence

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA China revenue (exportControlExposure) recovers above $9,000 million in a single quarter.
entries.mechanism: License caps and tariffs shut NVIDIA and AMD out of most China datacenter sales, so a large block of potential demand cannot be served.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to US license policy and China approvals.
entries.statement: Export-control policy materially constrains addressable merchant-GPU demand.
entries.title: Export control exposure

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA vendor-financed GPU commitments (rpoBacklog) shrink below the roughly 210,000 units across Firmus and Sharon AI, or the revenue-share program is wound down within two quarters.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA lends to and guarantees payment for the clouds that buy its chips, so part of its reported demand rests on credit it extends itself.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to NVIDIA's willingness to extend credit and to the solvency of the financed clouds.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's own revenue-sharing and credit-support financing increasingly underwrites its accelerator demand, coupling reported demand strength to NVIDIA-extended credit and adding circularity risk to buildout durability.
entries.title: Vendor-financed demand circularity

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Top-customer share of NVIDIA revenue (customerConcentration) falls below the prior 12/11/11 pattern, or the top three drop under 21/17/16 percent.
entries.mechanism: A handful of buyers make up more than half of NVIDIA's sales, so its revenue tilts further toward a few customers whose choices swing demand.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to hyperscaler order behavior and any shift to in-house silicon.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's data-center revenue is increasingly concentrated in a few direct customers, raising demand-durability risk if any single hyperscaler slows orders or shifts to in-house silicon.
entries.title: Customer concentration risk

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA loses its number-one datacenter Ethernet switch revenue rank (market-share-pct) within a year, or Spectrum-X attach to GPU sales falls quarter over quarter.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA sells its switches together with its GPUs, so buyers who want the full system stay with one supplier and face higher costs to switch away.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to networking attach rates and competing switch offerings.
entries.statement: NVIDIA now leads the data-center Ethernet switch market and sells that networking bundled with its GPUs, deepening a full-system moat that raises switching costs versus standalone accelerators and custom chips. NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching in Q1 2026 by pairing Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, and its NVLink systems cut cost per token, but no finding compares networking growth against compute growth, so the sharper claim that networking scales faster than compute is not supported.
entries.title: Networking attach as systems moat

entries.falsifiableTrigger: A new NVIDIA generation fails to lower cost per token (flopsPerDollar), or AMD's per-dollar hardware edge turns into inference share gains within a year.
entries.mechanism: Each new NVIDIA generation lowers the price to run a given AI query, so more inference workloads become affordable and move onto its chips.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to real-world cost per query versus vendor claims and to rival price-performance.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's each-generation drop in cost per token pulls more inference workloads onto its GPUs and widens the market it can serve.
entries.title: Cheaper inference pulls in demand

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Any of the 16 take-or-pay memory contracts (rpoBacklog) is cancelled or its deposits refunded within the 5-year term.
entries.mechanism: Buyers put billions in non-refundable deposits on multi-year memory contracts, so they are committed to build the accelerators that use that memory.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to contract enforceability and buyer solvency.
entries.statement: Non-cancelable multi-year HBM contracts backed by billions in customer deposits lock a large share of future accelerator demand into place and reduce the chance that today's buildout is speculative.
entries.title: Prepaid HBM take-or-pay locks in accelerator demand

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Approved China licenses (exportControlExposure) lift China revenue above the sub-50 percent cap, or the case-by-case window reverts to denial within two quarters.
entries.mechanism: US rules allow only reduced-capability chips into China under a volume cap, so sales can restart but cannot exceed a fraction of US demand.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the pace of license approvals and Chinese buyer follow-through.
entries.statement: China datacenter compute is reopening to NVIDIA only through case-by-case H200- and MI325-class licenses capped under half of comparable US sales, with Chinese buyers now signaling first purchases. This cycle adds H200-class licensing shifted from denial to case-by-case and buyer interest from Alibaba, ByteDance and DeepSeek, broadening the earlier MI325-only picture, while China revenue sits at $4,550M and H200 licenses have booked no revenue yet.
entries.title: Conditional China re-opening with a capped ceiling

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Additional frontier-scale models (S9) train on non-NVIDIA accelerators within a year, or AMD ROCm design wins (releaseCadence) accelerate.
entries.mechanism: Moving existing AI code off NVIDIA's software stack is costly, so many customers stay even when competing chips are cheaper.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the maturity of alternative software stacks and to forced substitution under export controls.
entries.statement: The difficulty of running existing AI software on non-Nvidia chips keeps customers on Nvidia even when rivals cut hardware prices.
entries.title: CUDA software lock-in sustains the moat

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AI-server memory bill-of-materials cost (S10) falls back toward baseline for 2 consecutive quarters, or DRAM output restrictions ease.
entries.mechanism: Memory and storage prices are climbing fast, so each AI server costs more to build and buyers face bigger bills.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the memory and storage supply-demand balance.
entries.statement: Fast-rising memory and NVMe prices are pushing up what it costs to build each AI server, which could strain buyer budgets and squeeze system margins.
entries.title: Rising memory costs inflate AI-server economics

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Any of Vera Rubin, MI450/Helios, Trainium 3, or Google TPU (releaseCadence) slips mass production past the second half of 2026, or memory supply (leadTimes) throttles the combined ramp.
entries.mechanism: Four major suppliers ramp new accelerators at the same time in the second half of 2026, so total available compute expands across vendors.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to memory and packaging supply throttling the combined ramp.
entries.statement: A simultaneous mass-production ramp of NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google accelerators is set to expand total accelerator supply in the second half of 2026.
entries.title: Multi-vendor accelerator supply wave in second-half 2026

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AMD customer concentration (customerConcentration) eases to a broad base, or one large deal cancellation cuts datacenter revenue (D2) in a quarter.
entries.mechanism: A few very large customer commitments drive most of AMD's accelerator growth, so its upside and downside both ride on a handful of partners.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the two anchor customers keep their commitments.
entries.statement: AMD's data-center accelerator growth depends on a small set of very large commitments, which concentrates both its upside and its downside in a handful of partners.
entries.title: AMD growth leans on a few mega-deals

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Crescent Island (S9) misses its second-half 2026 sampling, or its LPDDR5X design still needs the same advanced-packaging capacity as HBM parts.
entries.mechanism: Intel's inference chip swaps scarce high-bandwidth memory for standard memory, so it can be built without competing for tight advanced-packaging supply.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the sampling timeline and the chip's real packaging needs.
entries.statement: Intel's Crescent Island inference GPU uses standard LPDDR5X memory instead of high-bandwidth memory to sidestep the advanced-packaging and memory bottleneck, opening an alternative supply route for inference chips.
entries.title: Memory-based inference chips route around the packaging bottleneck

entries.falsifiableTrigger: TSMC wafer or advanced-packaging capacity (S10) runs slack, or foundry prices stay flat to down over two quarters despite steady AI orders.
entries.mechanism: AI orders now span many chip types, so TSMC's factories stay full and it can charge more across nodes.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the breadth of AI chip demand across nodes.
entries.statement: AI demand spreading across GPUs, custom ASICs, networking and power chips fills TSMC's capacity across nodes and lets it raise prices.
entries.title: Broadening AI demand tightens TSMC capacity and pricing

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler AI capex (D2) growth reverses from the 67 percent jump toward $650 billion, or order cancellations appear within two quarters.
entries.mechanism: Cloud giants spend far more on AI than they earn back so far, so weak returns could trigger a spending cut that reduces chip orders.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether AI revenue and returns keep pace with spending.
entries.statement: Hyperscalers' sharp jump in AI capital spending risks a pullback if returns disappoint, which would cut accelerator orders.
entries.title: Hyperscaler AI spending may outrun near-term returns

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA non-GAAP gross margin (grossMargin) falls below 70 percent for two consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Buyers keep paying top prices for NVIDIA's newest chips, so its profit margin stays near three-quarters even during a product ramp.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to pricing power versus the cost of ramping new products and rising memory prices.
entries.statement: NVIDIA keeps its gross margin near three-quarters even while ramping new products, far above rivals, showing buyers still pay premium prices.
entries.title: NVIDIA holds a premium gross margin as it ramps new chips

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Top three advanced-packaging clients' share (S10) drops below 70 percent within a year, or advanced-packaging lead times fall under 20 weeks.
entries.mechanism: A few buyers, led by NVIDIA, take the large majority of TSMC's advanced-packaging slots, so any one pulling back leaves capacity that is hard to refill.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to how concentrated packaging demand stays among the top buyers.
entries.statement: A few AI chip buyers control most of TSMC's advanced-packaging output, so any one of them cutting orders would leave capacity that is hard to refill.
entries.title: AI packaging supply is concentrated in a few buyers

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Weighted-average remaining life of supplier order books (rpoBacklog) shortens for two consecutive quarters, or the $21 billion and $73 billion multi-year commitments are cut.
entries.mechanism: Customers lock in multi-year compute purchases, so suppliers carry order books that stretch further into the future and steady near-term demand.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to customer solvency and willingness to hold long-dated commitments.
entries.statement: Cloud and chip suppliers are signing longer-dated purchase commitments that push out the average life of their order books, so a growing share of AI compute demand is booked years in advance.
entries.title: Backlog duration lengthens as demand is booked years ahead

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Leading model-provider run-rate revenue (apiArr) falls or stalls for two consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: As model makers add paying customers their revenue climbs quickly, so they can fund large compute purchases from real income.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the durability of AI application revenue growth.
entries.statement: Leading AI model providers are growing revenue fast enough that their heavy compute spending rests on real and rising sales rather than hope.
entries.title: Model-provider revenue scales to underpin the buildout
